stem: What is the greatest common divisor of [[m]] and [[n]]?
solution: Factoring [[m]] and [[n]] into primes and taking the shared prime powers at their lowest exponent gives \( \gcd([[m]],[[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the LEAST COMMON MULTIPLE of the two numbers, not their greatest common divisor.
This is the product of the two numbers, not their greatest common divisor.
This is the absolute difference of the two numbers, unrelated to their greatest common divisor.
This is off by one from the correct greatest common divisor.
This is off by one from the correct greatest common divisor.
This doubles the correct greatest common divisor for no valid reason.

stem: What is the least common multiple of [[m]] and [[n]]?
solution: Factoring [[m]] and [[n]] into primes and taking each prime power at its highest exponent gives \( \operatorname{lcm}([[m]],[[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the GREATEST COMMON DIVISOR of the two numbers, not their least common multiple.
This is the product of the two numbers; this only equals the LCM when the numbers are coprime.
This is the sum of the two numbers, unrelated to their least common multiple.
This adds an unrelated adjustment to the correct least common multiple.
This halves the correct least common multiple for no valid reason.
This subtracts an unrelated adjustment from the correct least common multiple.

solution: For each prime, the gcd takes the SMALLER of the two exponents (0 if a prime is absent from one factorization): \( \gcd(M,N) = 2^{\min([[a1]],[[a2]])}\cdot 3^{\min([[b1]],[[b2]])} = [[answer]] \) (the primes 5 and 7 each appear in only one number, so they contribute nothing to the gcd).
distractor_rationales: This is the least common multiple of \( M \) and \( N \), which uses the LARGER exponent of each prime, not the smaller.
This mistakenly uses the larger exponent of each shared prime instead of the smaller.
This is the product \( M\cdot N \), not their greatest common divisor.
This is off by one from the correct greatest common divisor.
This is off by one from the correct greatest common divisor.
This doubles the correct greatest common divisor for no valid reason.

stem: Let \( M = 2^{[[a1]]}\cdot 3^{[[b1]]} \) and \( N = 2^{[[a2]]}\cdot 5^{[[c2]]} \). What is \( \operatorname{lcm}(M, N) \)?
solution: For each prime, the lcm takes the LARGER of the two exponents: \( \operatorname{lcm}(M,N) = 2^{\max([[a1]],[[a2]])}\cdot 3^{[[b1]]}\cdot 5^{[[c2]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the greatest common divisor of \( M \) and \( N \), which uses the SMALLER exponent of each prime, not the larger.
This mistakenly uses the smaller exponent of the shared prime 2 instead of the larger.
This is the product \( M\cdot N \); this only equals the LCM when \( M \) and \( N \) are coprime.
This is off by one from the correct least common multiple.
This is off by one from the correct least common multiple.
This halves the correct least common multiple for no valid reason.

stem: A rectangular floor measuring [[L]] inches by [[W]] inches is to be tiled with the largest possible identical square tiles, with no tile cut and no gaps. What is the side length, in inches, of each square tile?
solution: The largest square tile that evenly divides both dimensions has side length equal to the greatest common divisor of the two dimensions: \( \gcd([[L]],[[W]]) = [[answer]] \) inches.
distractor_rationales: This is the least common multiple of the dimensions, not their greatest common divisor.
This is the total floor area, not a tile side length.
This is the difference of the two dimensions, unrelated to the tile size.
This is off by one from the correct tile side length.
This is off by one from the correct tile side length.
This doubles the correct tile side length for no valid reason.

stem: Two satellites pass over a tracking station every [[a]] days and every [[b]] days, respectively. Both satellites pass over the station today. In how many days will both satellites next pass over the station on the same day?
solution: Both satellites coincide again after a number of days that is a multiple of both [[a]] and [[b]]; the soonest such day is \( \operatorname{lcm}([[a]],[[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the greatest common divisor of the two periods, not their least common multiple.
This is the product of the two periods; this only equals the LCM when the periods are coprime.
This is the sum of the two periods, unrelated to when they next coincide.
This adds an unrelated adjustment to the correct least common multiple.
This subtracts an unrelated adjustment from the correct least common multiple.
This halves the correct least common multiple for no valid reason.
